summary.brief: Lynch v. State. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The plaintiffs, L and M, individually and in their representative capacities as parents of their minor son, J, and as the administrators of the estate of their daughter, S, sought to recover damages for the alleged medical malpractice of the named defendant, the state of Connecticut, in connection with certain therapeutic donor insemination (TDI) services and prenatal care provided to them at a state hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: M, who had tested negative for CMV, underwent a successful intrauterine insemination procedure using sperm from a CMV positive donor and became pregnant with J and S.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When M was twenty-two weeks pregnant, she underwent an ultrasound that revealed that J and S displayed conditions associated with an in utero CMV infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Subsequently, it was discovered that S had died in utero from a severe CMV infection, and J was born with debilitating, lifelong medical conditions as a result of congenital CMV.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Prior to trial, the plaintiffs disclosed that their causation expert, E, would testify that, to a reasonable degree of medical probability, the use of sperm from a CMV positive donor to inseminate M caused the CMV infection that resulted in S’s death and J’s severe injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiffs' expert

evidence_index.text: The trial court ultimately found in favor of the plaintiffs on thirteen counts and awarded them economic and noneconomic damages.
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court

evidence_index.text: The trial court denied the motion to dismiss, concluding that the plaintiffs’ fertility treatment claims were hybrid claims because, in the field of reproductive endocrinology, it is medical malpractice to inseminate a CMV negative patient with CMV positive donor sperm except when the patient is appropriately counseled as to the risks and gives her informed consent.
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court

evidence_index.text: The state could not prevail on its claim that the judgment should be set aside on the ground that the claims on which the plaintiffs prevailed at trial were barred by sovereign immunity.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When permission to sue the state is granted pursuant to § 4-160 (b), it applies to medical malpractice claims only, and, although medical malpractice and lack of informed consent are distinct causes of action, they are not mutually exclusive, and the same set of facts can give rise to both types of claims.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The fertility treatment claims brought by the plaintiffs on J’s behalf were properly construed and adjudicated as conventional medical malpractice claims and not, as the state contended, as wrongful life claims.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In the present case, all of those considerations weighed in favor of holding the state liable for the full amount of J’s economic and noneconomic damages proximately caused by the state’s negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: M, who had tested negative for CMV, underwent a successful intrauterine insemination procedure using sperm from a CMV positive donor and became pregnant with twins, J and S.
critical_findings: Use of CMV positive donor sperm

reveal.result_summary: Ultrasound revealed conditions associated with in utero CMV infection
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicated potential CMV infection in fetuses
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted further testing for CMV

requestables.name: CMV Serology
requestables.description: Serology tests to detect CMV infection

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: M presents for fertility treatment and becomes pregnant

key_events.event: Insemination with CMV positive donor sperm

decision_points.clinical_context: M is pregnant with twins after insemination with CMV positive donor sperm
decision_points.prompt: What should be done after discovering the ultrasound findings?

options.description: Perform follow-up tests to rule out CMV infection
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care requires ruling out CMV infection given the ultrasound findings

options.description: Do not perform any follow-up tests

expected_action_court.description: Follow-up tests should have been performed to rule out CMV infection
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated necessity of follow-up testing

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to perform follow-up tests after ultrasound findings
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Follow-up tests to confirm or rule out CMV infection
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Shay died in utero from severe CMV infection, and Joshua was born with severe injuries

legal_outcome.damages_awarded: Significant economic and noneconomic damages awarded
key_findings: Breach of duty in using CMV positive donor sperm
Causation established - CMV infection linked to donor sperm

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Use of CMV positive donor sperm
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Using CMV negative sperm would have prevented infection

factual_timeline.event: M inseminated with CMV positive donor sperm
Insemination with CMV positive donor sperm

tests_performed.result: Conditions associated with in utero CMV infection
IgM antibody level was 51.5 at the time of delivery

complications.complication: S died in utero from severe CMV infection

allegations.allegation: Failure to perform follow-up tests after ultrasound findings
Failure to perform follow-up testing after abnormal ultrasound
Use of CMV positive donor sperm

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by failing to perform follow-up tests

court_findings.legal_significance: Breach of standard of care by using CMV positive sperm
